result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only exists just before something changes. It isn’t the peaceful quiet of a lazy afternoon or the empty quiet of a room everyone has left. It’s tighter than that, like the air is holding its breath. You don’t always notice it in the moment. Most people only recognize it afterward, when they try to trace back the exact point where everything tipped.

I think about that kind of quiet whenever I remember the summer I almost left.

It was one of those long, heat-soaked seasons where the days blur together, stitched by the same sunlight and the same aimless routines. The town felt suspended, as if it had been set down gently and forgotten. The paint on the houses peeled in thin curls. Lawns grew uneven and stubborn. Even the stray dogs seemed too tired to bark. Nothing urgent ever happened there, which was exactly why I had begun to feel like I might dissolve into it if I stayed.

Every morning, I would wake up to the sound of the neighbor’s screen door slamming. It had a rhythm to it, a kind of unintentional clock. Open, pause, slam. Open, pause, slam. I never saw the neighbor, not clearly, just glimpses—a shoulder, the edge of a shirt, the suggestion of a life proceeding in parallel to mine. That felt important somehow, that invisible proximity. It made me wonder how many lives we brush against without ever really touching.

I had a plan, or at least I liked to call it that. It wasn’t detailed. It didn’t have contingencies or deadlines. It was more like a direction: away. Away from the repetition, away from the sense that my days were stacking neatly into something I hadn’t chosen. I imagined a bus, or a train, or even just a long road that didn’t circle back. I imagined stepping onto it and feeling, immediately, different. Lighter. Defined by motion instead of stillness.

But imagination is generous in ways reality is not.

The afternoon I nearly left, the sky was a washed-out blue, the kind that looks bleached rather than bright. I packed a bag with things that felt symbolic more than necessary: a notebook I hadn’t written in, a shirt I liked but never wore, a photograph of nothing in particular—just a street corner, taken on a day I couldn’t quite remember. I left my room without turning back, which felt dramatic in a way I couldn’t fully justify.

The bus station was smaller than I expected. Or maybe I had just built it up in my head, turned it into a kind of threshold. In reality, it was a squat building with flickering lights and a vending machine that hummed louder than it should have. There were only a few people there, each one wrapped in their own reasons for leaving or arriving. No one looked at anyone else for very long.

I bought a ticket without thinking too hard about the destination. That felt important—like overthinking might collapse the whole thing. The clerk barely glanced at me. The transaction took less than a minute. It struck me then how easy it is, mechanically, to change your life. A few words, a small exchange of money, and suddenly you’re pointed somewhere else.

I sat down to wait.

That’s when the quiet started.

Not the literal kind—the vending machine was still humming, someone was tapping their foot, a distant announcement crackled through old speakers—but underneath it, there was that tension. That sense of something balancing on a fine edge. I realized, sitting there with my bag at my feet, that nothing had changed yet. I was still the same person who had woken up that morning. The same thoughts were still circling. The same uncertainties.

Leaving, it turned out, wasn’t a clean break. It was more like carrying everything with you, just in a different direction.

I watched the door as people came and went. A woman argued quietly into her phone. A kid dragged a suitcase that seemed too big for him. An older man sat perfectly still, hands folded, as if he had been waiting for something much longer than a bus. Everyone seemed suspended in their own version of that pre-change quiet, whether they recognized it or not.

I tried to imagine my future as something distinct, something that would begin the moment I stepped onto the bus. But every version I came up with felt suspiciously similar to my present, just rearranged. Different streets, different faces, but the same underlying questions. The same restlessness.

That was the first crack in my plan.

The second came more quietly. I reached into my bag and pulled out the photograph—the one I had packed for reasons I couldn’t explain. It showed a street corner at dusk, the light caught in that brief moment where everything looks softer than it really is. There were no people in it, just buildings and shadows. At the time I took it, I must have thought it meant something. Holding it there in the bus station, I realized I couldn’t remember why.

It wasn’t a symbol of anything. It wasn’t a reminder of a specific moment or a specific feeling. It was just… a place I had been. A place I had once stood, long enough to notice the light and press a button.

That’s when it hit me, not as a dramatic revelation but as a slow, uncomfortable understanding: I had been waiting for meaning to announce itself to me, as if it were something external. As if changing my location would automatically change the weight of things.

But meaning, if it existed at all, seemed to come from attention. From the act of noticing, of staying long enough for something to matter. I hadn’t been doing that. I had been skimming across my own life, then blaming the scenery for feeling thin.

The bus arrived with a hiss of brakes that sounded almost like a sigh. People stood up, gathered their things, moved with that quiet urgency of departure. For a moment, I stood too. It felt like the expected thing to do, the continuation of a decision I had already set in motion.

Then I stopped.

It wasn’t fear, exactly. Or not only fear. It was the recognition that leaving and staying were not opposites in the way I had imagined. You could leave and still remain unchanged. You could stay and, if you paid enough attention, become someone else entirely.

The door of the bus opened. The driver glanced out, indifferent. The line moved forward.

I stepped back.

No one noticed. That felt significant in its own way. The world didn’t pause or question my choice. The bus didn’t wait. It filled, the door closed, and it pulled away, carrying with it a version of my life that would never exist.

The quiet broke.

Or maybe it didn’t break so much as dissolve into something less tense, more ordinary. The vending machine kept humming. The lights kept flickering. The same people were still there, minus a few. Everything looked almost exactly as it had before, which felt strangely different now.

I picked up my bag and walked out of the station.

The air outside was still heavy with heat, but it felt more breathable somehow. The town hadn’t changed. The peeling paint, the uneven lawns, the slow drift of days—it was all still there. But as I walked back, I noticed things I hadn’t before. The way the shadows stretched across the sidewalk. The faint sound of a radio playing somewhere behind an open window. The neighbor’s screen door, opening and slamming, opening and slamming, marking time in its own stubborn way.

Nothing had transformed. Not dramatically. Not in a way anyone else could see.

But the quiet before the change—that had passed. And in its place was something less defined, but more alive. Not the certainty of a new direction, but the willingness to stay long enough to find one.

I didn’t leave that summer.

But something did, quietly and without announcement: the idea that change only happens somewhere else.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
